summary: Gruber responds to Germany's Bundeskartellamt opening an investigation into Apple's App Tracking Transparency framework for alleged self-preferencing. He argues the regulators fundamentally misunderstand ATT: Apple's own apps don't show the tracking prompt not because Apple exempted itself, but because Apple doesn't engage in cross-app tracking. He contends that Apple deliberately chose not to build a surveillance advertising business despite the enormous revenue potential, and that ATT simply leveled the playing field by curtailing privacy-invasive tracking. The growth of Apple's Search Ads post-ATT is an indirect benefit, not proof of anti-competitive intent. Gruber frames surveillance advertising as cheating and ATT as an overdue correction.
key_points: The Bundeskartellamt's complaint rests on the claim that ATT rules apply to third parties but not Apple itself, which Gruber says misunderstands that Apple's apps genuinely don't track users across apps.
Around 70-75% of iOS users opt out of tracking when given the choice, which naturally benefits non-tracking ad platforms including Apple's Search Ads.
If Apple wanted to maximize ad revenue, it would have simply engaged in surveillance advertising itself rather than building costly privacy technologies like iCloud Private Relay.
German publishers like Axel Springer are pushing back against privacy measures broadly, suggesting the regulatory environment reflects entrenched surveillance-ad business interests.
Gruber frames the entire surveillance ad industry as systematic cheating, and ATT as a move toward a more level playing field.
money_quotes: It's like pawn shops suing to keep the police from cracking down on a wave of burglaries.
Apple's own apps don't show the track-you-across-other-apps permission alert not because Apple has exempted itself but because Apple's own apps don't track you across other apps.
Centuries of pre-internet advertising prove that tracking isn't necessary for advertising to work, but no one is arguing that tracking isn't effective.
If Apple actually cared more about maximizing Search Ads revenue than it does user privacy, wouldn't they have just engaged in actual user tracking?

The Bundeskartellamt perspective here completely disregards the idea that surveillance advertising is inherently unethical and Apple has studiously avoided it for that reason, despite the fact that it has proven to be wildly profitable for large platforms.
Apple could have made an enormous amount of money selling privacy-invasive ads on iOS, but opted not to.
I'd go so far as to describe the fundamental foundation of the surveillance ad industry as cheating.
The heretofore cheaters are griping that their advantage has been taken away.
key_insight: ATT didn't give Apple an unfair advantage — it removed the unfair advantage that surveillance advertisers had been exploiting, and Apple's indirect benefit is a consequence of never having cheated in the first place.
